Field Study · Issue 02
4 Days Until Lockout.
Seattle CBD · Class A · 135,890 RSF
A Live Field Study

A Class A Seattle asset.
A 3.66% variance no review caught.

Five years into the hold. Four days into the inspection window. The building had a verified rentable baseline at acquisition. It had not been re-tested since.

This is what the buyer's team found when they asked the question — and how a 4,973 SF gap became a $4.2 million valuation impact the new owner now operates against for the duration of the next hold.

135,890
Rentable SF · Per offering memorandum
3.66%
Variance at year five · Below review threshold
$4.2M
Valuation impact · At 6% blended cap rate

Chapter 02 · First Signal
Class A · Seattle CBD · 4 Days Until Lockout
From the Field

The review didn't start with the building.
It started with a window.

The asset was already under contract. Standard checks had cleared. The buyer's team didn't doubt the seller — they understood what happens if the rentable number is wrong and the deal closes against it.

That was the first signal. Not that the building was off — that the window to test it was about to close.

4 Days
Remaining in inspection window
5 Years
Since the RSF was last verified
"We have four days left in the inspection window. Who's verifying the RSF number before close?"

Chapter 03 · Field Verification
5 Events · 1 Asset · 1 Variance Pattern
From the Field

Every modification carried variance.
No single event carried it visibly.

Each modification across the five-year hold was independently verified against the current building condition. The pattern that emerged was consistent — and below review threshold on any single event.

Click any row to see how that event sat inside the 5% threshold most diligence reviews flag.

412 SF of unrecognized rentable area, billed against the wrong baseline from day one of the new lease. Reviewed alone, it cleared. The lease term locked it in for years.

Chapter 04 · Significance
5 Operating Events · 1 Compounding Variance
The Challenge

The variance wasn't hidden.
It was just out of time.

Three tenant expansions executed against the verified RSF baseline. One demising-line modification pulled from architect plans rather than the verified measurement file. Income compounded from those assumptions across five years of leases.

The asset wasn't operating in error. The window to fix it was just about to close.

Proximity to Revenue
Variance at year five

Each modification operated correctly within its own scope.

3.66%
Proximity to Revenue
Capturable rentable area unrecognized

The maintenance layer reset between events was missing.

4,973 SF
"You don't create revenue at lease signing. You freeze it."

Chapter 05 · The Window
The bilateral phase · Before close vs. after close
The Bilateral Phase

Before close, the seller has a stake in resolving it.
After close, you own it alone.

The inspection window isn't only about time — it's about who shares the math. Inside the window, a variance is a negotiated number. The seller can be asked to true up the rent roll, adjust the price, or warrant the count. Both sides have reason to land on the truth.

Outside the window, that bilateralism is gone. The seller has moved on. The number you bought is the number you operate against, and the only path back to accuracy runs through the leases themselves — measurement at renewal, demising at turnover, re-validation at expansion. Recovery is no longer a transaction. It's a cycle.

Pre-Close
Negotiated math · Bilateral leverage
Post-Close
Lease-cycle recovery · 5–10 years
"The window isn't a deadline.
It's the last moment the seller still has to care."
Ch. 05 · Live
The Recovery Window
Tap each phase to see what's available.
~45
Days of bilateral leverage
Pre-Close
30–60 days
CLOSE
Post-Close

Inside the inspection window, the variance is negotiated — both parties have a stake in resolving it before the period expires.

Chapter 06 · When It Surfaces
Key drivers · Why the variance went unseen
Key Drivers

No single change failed.
The system never re-tested itself.

The 3.66% variance didn't arrive in a single event. It accumulated. Tenant expansions, demising work, suite turnovers — each one looked clean against the documents in front of it. Each one was approved, executed, and filed.

What was missing was the layer in between: the return to the verified baseline. No event triggered a re-measurement. Every revision wrote against the prior figure, not the field. Over five years of compounding, the drift became the building.

By the time it surfaced, the bilateral window was closing. Pre-close, the variance is a price negotiation — three or four days of fieldwork resolves it, and whatever the answer, it gets priced in. Post-close, that mechanism is gone. The only path back to accurate rentable area runs through lease renewal cycles, and office leases run five to seven years.

"Each event was clean. The system that should have caught the drift between events was the part that wasn't there."
Ch. 06 · Reference
The Four Drivers
How a documented building drifts 3.66% without a single failed check.
  1. 01
    Variance Below The Threshold
    No single modification carried a variance large enough to trigger review on its own. Each event looked clean. Across five years of compounding, it became the building.
  2. 02
    The Maintenance Layer Was Missing
    Each modification was documented, approved, and executed. None of them routed back to the verified baseline. The number was never re-tested between events.
  3. 03
    The Window Is Bilateral
    Pre-close, the variance is a price negotiation. Both parties have access to it. Three or four days of fieldwork resolves the question, and whatever the answer, it gets priced in.
  4. 04
    Once The Window Closes, So Does The Reset
    There is no mechanism for the new owner to reset rentable area on an existing lease. The only path back is the renewal. Office leases run five to seven years.

Chapter 07 · Impact
The valuation consequence · $4.2M frozen at close
The Verdict

3.66% of the building was unrecognized.
$4.2M of value was frozen with the close.

The variance, valued at the asset's own cap rate, produced a one-time valuation impact of $4.2 million. Not theoretical. Not market-dependent. Math the new owner now operates against for the duration of the hold.

Annually, the same variance represents $253,000 in rentable area the building generates but the rent roll doesn't capture. Across the 5-year hold, that's $1.27M in compounding unrecognized revenue — money the asset produces but the lease structure can't bill against until each suite turns.

$4.2M
One-time valuation impact
$253K
Annual unrecognized revenue
$1.27M
5-year compounding total
"The variance wasn't a hypothetical.
It was a price the new owner paid for a building that included 4,973 SF nobody was renting."

Chapter 08 · Prevention
Eight Conditions · Matched Actions
Prevention · Reference

Where verification still has leverage.

The variance was the same one every operating event missed. The window to find it first is the window before someone else does. Tap any condition that applies to a building you're working on — the matched action set compiles below.

  1. Verified rentable baseline older than 24 months

    The number was correct at acquisition. Every additional month compounds the gap.

    Remeasure
  2. Single unverified RSF figure carrying every lease

    When one number propagates across the rent roll, OM, and underwriting, the variance flows through every downstream calculation.

    Remeasure
  3. Conflicting figures across plan sets, rent rolls, and OMs

    Inconsistency isn't a documentation problem. It's evidence the baseline isn't being maintained.

    Reconcile
  4. Tenant expansion executed without re-verification

    Each expansion creates new variance against the baseline. Across a five-year hold, those variances compound.

    Verify
  5. Demising-line modification pulled from architect plans

    Architect plans aren't the verified measurement file. The lease and the building reflect different things.

    Recalculate
  6. Suite turnover without re-measurement at vacancy

    Vacancy is the single moment the number can be tested without disrupting an active lease.

    Verify
  7. Asset under contract, inspection window open

    Pre-close, the variance is a price negotiation. Three or four days of fieldwork resolves the question.

    Remeasure
  8. Disposition planned within 24 months

    The number on the rent roll today is effectively the number that closes the deal.

    Remeasure
Matched Actions

Select any condition above to compile a quick action set.

    "4 days is the window.
    After that, recovery runs in lease cycles."
